parsed.summary: The cumulative v0.4.0→v0.4.6 diff introduces +6600 lines across 84 files with critical new infrastructure (retry logic, error dispatch, network client changes) but leaves the highest-risk integration point—glm-client.mjs, the core network client that wires retry.mjs and bigmodel-errors.mjs together—entirely without dedicated test coverage. Additionally, process.mjs was deleted and tracked-jobs.mjs + job-control.mjs were modified without corresponding tests, creating blind spots where silent failures in job lifecycle state management would be undetectable until production. This should not ship until glm-client.mjs integration paths are covered.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs heavily modified but has zero dedicated test coverage
findings.body: scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs shows 155 lines changed—the largest modification to any existing lib module. It is the integration point that wires the new retry.mjs and bigmodel-errors.mjs into actual API calls. Yet no tests/glm-client.test.mjs exists in the test directory. Retry behavior, backoff timing, error classification cascading through to the client, and idempotency of retried requests are all untested. A misclassification in bigmodel-errors.mjs that causes glm-client.mjs to retry a non-idempotent mutation (or silently swallow a real failure) would be invisible until a user hits it.
findings.recommendation: Add tests/glm-client.test.mjs covering: (a) retry on retriable BigModel errors, (b) no-retry on non-retriable errors, (c) max-retry exhaustion behavior, (d) backoff timing, (e) request idempotency when retries fire. At minimum, mock the HTTP layer and verify the retry×error-classification integration end-to-end.

findings.title: process.mjs deleted—surviving imports would cause runtime MODULE_NOT_FOUND
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs (67 lines) was fully deleted per the diff stat. Other files in scripts/lib/ were modified in the same span (job-control.mjs, glm-client.mjs, render.mjs). If any surviving import path references './process.mjs' or './lib/process', the failure is a hard crash at import time. Without seeing the full file contents I cannot confirm all call sites were migrated, but the risk is a load-time failure that cannot be caught by tests that don't exercise the specific import path.
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase for any remaining references to 'process.mjs' or 'lib/process'. If the functionality was inlined elsewhere, verify every former consumer was updated. Add a CI check that validates all imports resolve (e.g., node --check or a static import analysis).

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs and job-control.mjs modified without dedicated test files
findings.body: scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) and scripts/lib/job-control.mjs (20 lines changed) manage job lifecycle state—creation, tracking, completion. Neither has a dedicated test file (no tests/tracked-jobs.test.mjs or tests/job-control.test.mjs). These modules sit between the new retry/error infrastructure and the companion CLI. A state corruption bug (e.g., a retried job being tracked twice, or a completed job not being cleaned up) would silently accumulate state until a downstream failure that is very difficult to diagnose.
findings.recommendation: Add tests for tracked-jobs.mjs and job-control.mjs covering: job creation, duplicate detection, cleanup on completion, and state after partial failure/retry. These are small modules—test coverage should be straightforward and high-value.

next_steps: Add tests/glm-client.test.mjs with mocked HTTP covering the retry+error-classification integration before merging
Run grep -r 'process\.mjs' scripts/ to confirm no surviving imports to the deleted module
Add unit tests for tracked-jobs.mjs and job-control.mjs covering lifecycle state transitions under retry scenarios
Consider an integration smoke test that exercises the full companion flow (submit job → error → retry → success) to catch cross-module regressions
